1.exposition: The nightmare of the two beasts breaks off, and John lifts his eyes to a wholly different scene. Where the dragon had branded the earth's inhabitants with a mark, now the Lamb stands on Mount Zion, and with him the 144,000 — sealed not with the beast's name but with his own and his Father's. The persecuting power that seemed to fill the horizon is answered, point for point, by the triumphant Lamb and his redeemed company. From heaven comes a sound at once overwhelming and sweet — like many waters and thunder, yet like harpers at their harps — as the ransomed sing a new song that only the redeemed can learn. Then the company is described: undefiled, following the Lamb wherever he goes, the firstfruits of a great harvest, truthful and blameless before the throne. Every clause is the negative of the beast's world: those who refused the mark now bear the seal; those who would not lie for the beast have no lie in their mouth. The chapter opens, in short, by showing the reader which side has already won.
1.original_language: Two of the section's terms carry its weight. The redeemed are the aparchē (“firstfruits”) — in the Law the first and representative portion of a harvest, offered to God as the pledge of the whole (Leviticus 23:10; Numbers 15:20) — so the phrase either marks the 144,000 as the earnest of a fuller ingathering or consecrates the whole company as holy to God and the Lamb. They are amōmoi (“blameless,” v.5), the sacrificial word for an unblemished offering — the very term the doxology uses of the church God will present faultless (Jude 24). Their song is a kainē ōdē, a “new song” — new because it celebrates a new and greater redemption.
1.historical_context: Mount Zion is the prophets' eschatological gathering-place: God sets his King on Zion (Psalms 2:6), the LORD of hosts reigns there (Isaiah 24:23), and to Zion the nations stream in the last days (Isaiah 2:2–4; Micah 4:1–4). The Lamb's presence there fulfills those hopes in person. The 144,000 — twelve times twelve times a thousand — is the full, complete Israel of God, the sealed company of Revelation 7:3. And the seal on the forehead is the exact counter-image to the mark of the beast (Revelation 13:16): in a world where allegiance is stamped on the body, God stamps his own. The “new song no one could learn except the redeemed” echoes — and inverts — the secret knowledge prized by the mystery cults: this song is learned not by esoteric initiation but by the costly road of redemption.
1.christ: The one enthroned on Zion is not an abstract sovereign but the slain Lamb, and the community bears his name beside the Father's, its whole identity derived from his. The new song is the song of their own redemption — experiential knowledge, not theory; those who have not been bought cannot sing it. And “following the Lamb wherever he goes” is the Gospel call to discipleship (Mark 8:34: take up the cross and follow) enlarged to cosmic scope: those who follow him through death follow him onto Zion. The Lamb who alone was worthy to open the scroll now stands crowned among his people.

verses.note: The vision pivots. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that over against the beast and its worshippers John now sees the Lamb standing on Mount Zion with the 144,000 — the triumphant counterpart to the beast's dominion, Zion signifying the church, God's people, or the heavenly city. latin-fathers Bede reads it pastorally: while the Church toiled under the dragon's assault, the Lord Jesus preserved her by the example of his power and the defence of his protection. On their foreheads is the Father's name — the seal of God's ownership (Revelation 7:3), in pointed contrast to the beast's brand (Revelation 13:16): they are openly, unashamedly his.

The sound that accompanies them is described by paradox. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the two notes struck at once — a voice like many waters and a great thunder, all majesty and volume, yet also like harpers harping with their harps, all melody and sweetness. Heaven's worship is both mighty as the sea and the storm and tender as skilled music (cf. Revelation 5:8); the redeemed sing to the harp before the throne, power and beauty in one voice.

They sing a new song before the throne, the living creatures, and the elders — a “new song” (as in Revelation 5:9) because it celebrates the new and greater work of grace. grammatical-historical Barnes calls it the song peculiar to the redeemed; wesleyan-arminian Wesley presses the striking limit — none could learn it but the ransomed, for the experience of redemption is its very grammar. Those not bought from the earth cannot enter its meaning or its joy.

The company is now described, and the first phrase divides interpreters. grammatical-historical Robertson and Barnes take “not defiled with women, for they are virgins” chiefly as spiritual chastity — purity kept from the spiritual fornication of idolatry and beast-worship, the church as the chaste virgin betrothed to Christ (2 Corinthians 11:2); a minority read literal celibacy. wesleyan-arminian Wesley glosses them simply as “unspotted souls.” That they follow the Lamb “whithersoever he goeth” marks unreserved devotion — grammatical-historical Gill: following him in the exercise of grace, in his sufferings, in his ordinances and his very footsteps, wherever he leads. And they are the “firstfruits,” redeemed from among men and consecrated wholly to God and the Lamb.

The description closes on their truthfulness: in their mouth was found no lie, for they are blameless before the throne of God. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott hear the contrast with the beast and false prophet, whose whole power ran on deceit — the redeemed belong to the God of truth and speak it (cf. Zephaniah 3:13). “Blameless” (amōmos) is the language of an unblemished sacrifice: they stand faultless in God's presence, made so by the Lamb who ransomed them.

witnesses.point: While the Church toiled under the dragon's assault, Christ preserved her by his power and shielded her by his protection — the Lamb on Zion is the guarded, sealed people of God.
The vision turns from the persecuting beast to the triumphant Lamb; “not defiled with women” is best taken as spiritual purity kept from the idolatry of beast-worship.
The 144,000 are “unspotted souls” who follow the Lamb most nearly; the new song can be learned by none but the redeemed themselves.
To follow the Lamb “whithersoever he goeth” is to follow him in grace, in suffering, in his ordinances and his steps — a following general and extensive, taking in his whole conduct.

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.point: Reads the 144,000 as the whole redeemed community numbered as an army for holy war (12 × 12 × 1,000), and “undefiled by women” against the Old Testament purity required of soldiers on campaign — a metaphor of consecrated readiness and of freedom from the spiritual adultery of idolatry, not a literal celibate elite.

1.application: The chapter refuses to let the beast have the first word. Before a single judgment falls, John is shown the end of the story — the Lamb already standing, his people already sealed, their song already sung. Faithfulness under pressure is not a gamble on an uncertain future but alignment with a victory already secured.

6.exposition: Three angels now cross the sky in succession, and their messages together frame the choice the whole book presses. The first bears an eternal gospel — the only place Scripture joins those two words — and proclaims it to every nation, tribe, tongue, and people, summoning the earth to fear God, give him glory, and worship the Creator, “for the hour of his judgment has come.” The second announces a fall already as good as accomplished: “Babylon is fallen, is fallen,” the great seducer of the nations. The third utters the book's most terrible warning — that whoever worships the beast and takes his mark will drink the wine of God's wrath undiluted, and have no rest day or night. Set against that restlessness, a voice from heaven pronounces the second of Revelation's seven beatitudes: “Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord.” Between the terror and the blessing stands the hinge of the section — “Here is the endurance of the saints” — for it is precisely this contrast of destinies that steels the faithful to refuse the mark and, if need be, to die.
6.original_language: “Eternal gospel,” euangelion aiōnion, is good news as old and abiding as God himself, still proclaimed under the beast. “Fallen, fallen” is a prophetic perfect, epesen epesen — the ruin so certain it is announced as already past, the very words of the Greek Isaiah 21:9. In v.10 the wine of wrath is akratos, “unmixed”: where the ancients cut their wine with water and only the intemperate drank it neat, God's wrath is here served at full strength, unmitigated by mercy. And the beatitude opens with makarioi (“blessed,” v.13), sealed by the Spirit's emphatic “Yes” (nai).
6.historical_context: Babylon's fall borrows the very words of the prophets against the historic Babylon — Isaiah 21:9, Jeremiah 51:8 — now aimed at the great city of John's day and beyond her at every empire that seduces the nations with wealth and idolatry (unfolded in chapters 17–18). The imagery of drinking runs as a deliberate reversal: Babylon makes the nations drink the wine of her fornication (v.8); those who prefer her cup will drink instead the wine of God's wrath (v.10). The historicist expositors — Barnes, Gill, Henry — read Babylon as papal Rome; others as pagan Rome or the world-system. And the beatitude of v.13 answers the exact question the churches of Asia faced under the imperial cult and the trade guilds: is it better to compromise and survive, or to die in the Lord?
6.christ: The judgment of the beast-worshippers is not impersonal: it falls “in the presence of the Lamb” — before the very one whose blood was the alternative offered to every person. The eternal gospel calls the world to worship him who made heaven and earth, the sea and the springs; and in Revelation's theology the Creator's glory is shared by the Lamb through whom all things were made (cf. John 1:3; Colossians 1:16), so that to fear God is to bow to the Lamb enthroned. And “the dead who die in the Lord” die in union with the crucified and risen one — and therefore rest.

verses.note: An angel flies in mid-heaven with an eternal gospel to preach to every nation, tribe, tongue, and people. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the herald visible to all, bearing the everlasting good news even in the teeth of the beast's dominion; puritan Matthew Henry stresses that the gospel is “eternal” — unchanging, ordained before the world, and still preached when all else fails. The latin-fathers Victorinus read the flying angel as an Elijah-figure who heralds God's word before the brief reign of Antichrist — an older reading that hears in the scene a last summons of grace before the end.

The angel's cry is a call to true worship against false: “Fear God and give him glory... and worship him who made heaven and earth, the sea and the springs of water.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the ground of its urgency — “the hour of his judgment has come” — and the deliberate appeal to God as Creator: over against the beast and its image, the earth is summoned back to the One who made it. To fear the Creator is to renounce the creature that demands to be worshiped.

The second angel: “Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear the doubled verb of Isaiah 21:9 and Jeremiah 51:8 — the certainty and completeness of her ruin announced before it visibly occurs. The cause is her seduction of the nations, whom she made drink “the wine of her fornication.” grammatical-historical Gill and the historicist reading identify Babylon with the great persecuting power — papal Rome to many, pagan Rome or the world-system to others (drawn out in chapters 17–18). latin-fathers Victorinus links this following angel to the prophetic witness that accompanies the first.

The third angel warns, with a loud voice, against worshipping the beast and his image and receiving his mark in forehead or hand. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that the solemn condition is stated first, before the dreadful consequence is unfolded (vv.10–11). The choice is now nakedly set: the mark of the beast (Revelation 13:16) or the seal of God — worship the beast, or fear God and give him glory (v.7).

The consequence: he shall drink the wine of God's wrath, poured unmixed into the cup of his indignation. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson press the word akratos — full-strength wine, wrath undiluted by mercy, the biblical cup of anger handed to the nations (Psalms 75:8; Jeremiah 25:15). And the torment is “with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels and of the Lamb” — open, witnessed, before the very Lamb whose mercy was refused. The severity is deliberate: the warning is weighed to match the pressure the beast can bring.

The smoke of their torment ascends forever, and they have no rest day nor night. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the image drawn from Isaiah 34:10 — the perpetual rising smoke of a ruined land — marking the unceasing duration of the judgment. The phrase “no rest day nor night” is set, pointedly, against the rest promised two verses on (v.13): the beast's servants are restless; the Lamb's dead rest.

“Here is the endurance of the saints, those who keep the commandments of God and the faith of Jesus.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read this as the very point of the warning: the contrast of destinies is what sustains the saints to endure rather than yield. The two marks of the faithful — obedience to God's commandments and the holding of the faith of Jesus (as in Revelation 12:17) — identify those who, under the beast's pressure, stay loyal to God and to Christ.

Then, breaking in from heaven, a voice bids John write: “Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord from now on.” puritan Matthew Henry marks the solemn weight — the word comes down from heaven, not from men, and so is of certain truth and great authority; and he reads the promise that “their works follow them” not as the ground of salvation but as its fruit and reward, going with them into God's presence. The Spirit himself answers “Yes,” confirming that they rest from their labours — the very rest denied the beast-worshippers (v.11). This is the second of the book's seven beatitudes, and the most pastoral: to the persecuted, to die in the Lord is not loss but the beginning of rest.

witnesses.point: The wine “without mixture” is wrath at full strength: where wine was normally diluted, this cup is undiluted by mercy — the image of a punishment with nothing to mitigate it.
The beatitude on the faithful dead came down from heaven, not from men, and so is of certain truth; their works follow them as the evidence and reward of their faith.
Reads the angel flying in mid-heaven as an Elijah-figure heralding God's word before Antichrist's reign, the following angel as the associate prophet.
The smoke rising for ever (from Isaiah 34) marks the perpetual duration of the judgment; “no rest day nor night” stands over against the rest of those who die in the Lord.

external.name: Craig R. Koester
external.point: Sets the three messages against the pressures of Roman Asia: the imperial cult and the trade guilds made refusing “the beast” socially and economically costly, so the beatitude of v.13 answers a live dilemma — compromise and survive, or stay faithful and perhaps die — and the paired cups (Babylon's seductive wine, God's wine of wrath) set the two ways starkly side by side.

6.application: The three angels leave no neutral ground: every hearer is drinking from one cup or the other. The section's terror is real, but its last word is a blessing — placed exactly where the pressure is greatest — telling those who may lose their lives that they lose nothing that lasts, and gain rest.

14.exposition: The chapter closes with two reapings. First John sees one like a son of man on a white cloud, crowned with gold and holding a sharp sickle; an angel cries from the temple that the hour has come, and he swings his sickle and the earth is reaped. Then a second angel comes from the heavenly temple, also with a sharp sickle, and a third — the angel of the altar, with power over fire — calls him to gather the clusters of “the vine of the earth,” for its grapes are ripe. The vintage is cast into the great winepress of the wrath of God, trodden outside the city until blood flows to the horses' bridles for sixteen hundred stadia. The imagery is lifted whole from Joel 3:13 — “Put in the sickle, for the harvest is ripe; go in, tread, for the winepress is full.” Whether the two harvests picture one judgment from two angles, or distinguish the gathering of the elect (the grain) from the doom of the wicked (the grape), the point holds: history ripens, and at the appointed hour it is reaped.
14.original_language: Two distinct harvests stand behind the Greek. The first is the grain harvest, therismos, the reaping of a ripe field; the field is said to be xērainō — “dried,” over-ripe, past ready. The second is the vintage, the gathering of grapes for the lēnos, the trough where the fruit was trodden underfoot. The reaper's crown is a stephanos — the victor's wreath, the same word given the overcomer elsewhere in the book, not a mere royal diadem. And the sickle, drepanon, is named again and again across these verses, hammering a single note: the time for cutting has come.
14.historical_context: The whole scene is woven from the prophets' harvest of judgment. Joel 3:13 supplies both images at once — sickle-harvest and winepress — set in the Valley of Decision where the nations are judged. The treading “outside the city” and the blood-stained press recall Isaiah 63:1–6, where the LORD treads the winepress alone, his garments spattered with the lifeblood of the nations — the same picture that returns when the rider's robe is dipped in blood and he treads “the winepress of the fury of the wrath of God” (Revelation 19:15). Grapes “fully ripe” echo the principle that judgment waits until iniquity is complete (Genesis 15:16). The “sixteen hundred stadia” (about two hundred miles) is best read as a symbolic figure of completeness — four squared times one hundred — the judgment reaching to every quarter of the earth.
14.christ: The crowned reaper is “one like a son of man” — the figure of Daniel 7:13 who comes with the clouds of heaven and receives dominion over all peoples. In Revelation's unfolding, the Lamb who was worthy to open the scroll is now revealed as the Son of Man who reaps the harvest: the same person, a different office. His golden crown is the victor's wreath — the harvester is himself the Overcomer. And the harvest is the long-awaited answer to the martyrs' cry, “How long?” (Revelation 6:10): the Son of Man's coming-with-clouds, the hope of the persecuted since Daniel, at last arrives, and history is gathered in.

verses.note: A white cloud, and on it one like the Son of man, a golden crown on his head, a sharp sickle in his hand. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson identify him as Christ coming to reap, the white cloud marking the glory of his advent (cf. Revelation 1:7; Matthew 24:30); eastern Cyril of Jerusalem hears behind it Daniel's night-vision of one like the Son of Man coming with the clouds of heaven. grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the golden stephanos — the crown of royal victory. puritan Matthew Henry observes that a harvest can figure either the gathering of the righteous when ripe for heaven or the judgment of the wicked ripe for wrath; here the crowned Reaper comes.

An angel comes out of the temple, crying, “Put in your sickle and reap, for the hour has come and the harvest of the earth is ripe.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that the command issues from the temple — from God's own presence — so that the timing of the harvest is fixed by the Father, not seized by the reaper (cf. Matthew 24:36). The field is “ripe” — dried, past ready; the appointed moment has arrived.

He who sat on the cloud swung his sickle over the earth, and the earth was reaped. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the swift obedience to the appointed word — the ingathering carried out at once and completely. Whether it is the gathering of the wheat into the barn (Matthew 13:30) or the reaping of judgment, it is done at the Son of Man's own hand.

Another angel comes out of the temple in heaven, he also having a sharp sickle — the second reaper, now for the vintage. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that he too proceeds from the heavenly sanctuary, the source of the appointed judgment. latin-fathers Victorinus reads the sickle and the gathered grapes as the nations that perish at the Lord's advent — the consummation of the world and the coming of Christ's kingdom, shown, he says, under many figures.

A third angel, from the altar, having power over fire, calls to gather the clusters of the vine of the earth, for its grapes are fully ripe. grammatical-historical Ellicott hears the altar as the very place where the martyrs cried out (Revelation 6:9–10) and where incense was mingled with the prayers of the saints (Revelation 8:3–5) — so the vintage of wrath answers the saints' long prayer for vindication. grammatical-historical Barnes notes that “the vine of the earth” is the wicked, ripe in iniquity — over against the true Vine — gathered now for the press, their guilt come to full measure (cf. Joel 3:13).

The angel gathered the vine of the earth and cast it into the great winepress of the wrath of God. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the harvest of the ungodly, set in deliberate contrast to the ingathering of the earth two verses before (v.16): the same world yields two harvests. The winepress (Isaiah 63:3; Joel 3:13) is the ancient figure of God's judgment, where the wicked are trodden like grapes.

The winepress was trodden outside the city, and blood flowed from it to the horses' bridles for sixteen hundred stadia. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read the picture as deliberate hyperbole — an immense, unimaginable slaughter, blood where wine should run — conveying the totality of the judgment on the ungodly (the treading of Isaiah 63:3). “Outside the city” sets the doom beyond the holy place; the sixteen hundred stadia mark its universal reach. The press trodden here is trodden again by the rider whose robe is dipped in blood (Revelation 19:15).

witnesses.point: When warnings produce no reformation, the sins of the nations are filled up and they ripen for judgment — a harvest that figures both the gathering of the righteous for heaven and the cutting-down of the wicked.
The crowned figure on the white cloud is Christ, the Son of Man of Daniel 7, coming to reap; the command from the temple shows the harvest reaped at the Father's appointed hour.
Hears in the reaper on the cloud Daniel's vision of “one like the Son of Man” coming with the clouds of heaven — the Danielic figure whose appearing signals the consummation.
Reads the angel “who hath power over the fire” against the altar scenes of the martyrs' cry and the saints' prayers — the vintage of wrath answering their plea for vindication.

external.name: Robert H. Mounce
external.point: Takes the two harvests as complementary images of the final judgment — many reading the grain harvest as the ingathering and the grape harvest as the outpouring of wrath — and treats the blood rising to the horses' bridles for 1,600 stadia as calculated hyperbole for the completeness and horror of the judgment, not a literal measure.

14.application: Harvest is not arbitrary violence but ripening come to term: a field becomes what it grew. The same earth that filled with beast-worship and Babylon's seductions is here reaped for it. The image sobers — and steadies: the God who lets history ripen also fixes its hour, and the hour is his.

_meta.title: The Lamb on Zion and the Harvest of the Earth
reflection: The 144,000 bear the Lamb's name and the Father's on their foreheads, the exact counter to the beast's mark (v.1; Revelation 13:16). Whose name is written, visibly, over your allegiances — and where is the pressure to be marked otherwise?
Heaven calls “Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord” and promises that “their works follow them” (v.13). How does that beatitude change the arithmetic of what faithfulness costs you now?
Two harvests are reaped from one earth — grain and grape (vv.14–16; vv.19–20), drawn from Joel's Valley of Decision (Joel 3:13). What is ripening in your own life toward one harvest or the other?
